title: Overview
overview: JioOnePay is a **payment aggregation platform** built by Jio Platforms.
Merchants integrate with it once, instead of separately with every payment gateway or bank.
It sits directly **between the Merchant and the Payment Gateway/Bank** — the merchant only ever talks to JioOnePay, never to the bank directly.
It's a **microservices-based** system that routes, processes, and tracks payments across UPI, cards, wallets, and net banking.
why: A merchant that wants to accept UPI, cards, wallets, and net banking needs a separate integration for each one.
Every payment gateway has its own API, its own auth mechanism, and its own response format.
An aggregator absorbs all of that complexity once, so merchants integrate with a single, consistent API.
problem: Before aggregators, a merchant integrated **individually** with every gateway — high effort, high ongoing maintenance.
Adding a new payment method meant a brand-new integration project, not a config change.
There was no single place to monitor transactions, handle refunds, or retry failures across all payment methods.

intro: The architecture, stack, and my specific role in building this platform.

topics.heading: Application Architecture
points: **Microservices architecture** — each service is independently deployable, scalable, and easy to extend.
**API Gateway pattern** — `jop-gateway-service` is the single entry point for all traffic; it routes requests and handles resilience (circuit breaker, rate limiting).

topics.heading: Technologies Used
points: **Backend** — Core Java, Spring Boot, Spring Data JPA, Oracle Database, REST APIs.
**Frontend** — React, Next.js, TypeScript.

topics.heading: Key Features
points: Merchant Onboarding.
Payment Gateway Integration.
Multiple Payment Modes (UPI, Card, Wallet, Net Banking).
Dynamic Payment Routing.
Refunds & Transaction Monitoring.

topics.heading: My Roles & Responsibilities
points: Building REST APIs & business logic in Spring Boot, integrating new payment gateways, writing optimized Oracle SQL.
Frontend development in React/Next.js — building UI components and wiring them to backend APIs.
Code reviews, unit tests, debugging, and working with QA/Product/DevOps through release.

topics.heading: Challenges
points: **Problem** — integrating multiple payment gateways, each with different request/response formats and authentication mechanisms.
**Solution** — a common abstraction layer converts our internal request into the gateway-specific format, and transforms the gateway response back into our standard model.
**Impact** — merchants use a single API, and new gateways can be integrated without impacting existing merchants.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph LR
    A["Merchant"] --> B["JioOnePay"]
    B --> C["Payment Gateway"]
    C --> D["Bank"]
    D --> E["Response"]
architectureFlow.caption: The simplest possible view — JioOnePay sits between the merchant and the payment gateway/bank. The full flow is broken down layer by layer next.

simpleExample.scenario: A merchant's checkout page initiates a UPI payment through JioOnePay's REST API.
simpleExample.input: A merchant-initiated payment request for a fixed order amount, specifying UPI as the payment mode.
simpleExample.execution: JioOnePay validates the merchant and order, resolves UPI as the payment mode, and routes the request to the configured UPI-handling service/bank without the merchant needing to know which bank actually processes it.
simpleExample.output: A response containing a transactionId and a status (e.g. PENDING, moving to SUCCESS or FAILED once the bank responds).
simpleExample.explanation: The merchant only ever talks to one API — JioOnePay — regardless of which underlying bank or gateway actually settles the UPI transaction.

realWorldExample.scenario: Integrating multiple payment gateways, each with a different request/response format and authentication mechanism.
realWorldExample.input: An internal, standard payment request that must be sent to different gateways with completely different APIs.
realWorldExample.execution: Each gateway gets its own adapter implementing the same interface — converting the internal request into that gateway's specific format, and transforming its response back into JioOnePay's standard response model.
realWorldExample.output: Merchants and internal services always see the same standard request/response shape, regardless of which gateway actually handled the transaction.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is the Adapter design pattern applied at a system level — new gateways can be added by writing one new adapter class, without touching any existing merchant integration.

bestPractices: Build one common internal request/response model, and translate to/from each gateway's format only at the edges.
Keep gateway-specific quirks (auth, format, error codes) isolated inside that gateway's own adapter.
Centralize monitoring and refund handling at the aggregator layer for one consistent merchant experience.
commonMistakes: Letting one payment gateway's request/response shape leak into the shared internal domain model.
Hardcoding gateway-specific logic in shared services instead of isolating it behind an adapter.
Treating a new gateway integration as a special case instead of another implementation of the same adapter interface.
interviewTips: When asked "tell me about your project," lead with the business problem, then the solution.
Be ready to go deep on the Challenges section — interviewers often follow up on **how** you solved it, not just what you built.

faq.q: Why not let merchants integrate with each payment gateway directly?
faq.a: Every gateway has its own API, auth mechanism, and response format — pushing that complexity to every merchant multiplies the integration and maintenance cost across the whole ecosystem. An aggregator absorbs that complexity exactly once.

faq.q: What pattern solves the multi-gateway integration problem?
faq.a: An adapter layer: one standard internal request/response model, with a dedicated adapter class per gateway that converts to/from that gateway's specific format. Adding a gateway means adding one adapter, not touching existing code.

faq.q: What's the actual business impact of this abstraction?
faq.a: Merchants use a single, stable API regardless of how many gateways JioOnePay adds or changes behind the scenes — and new gateways can be onboarded without any risk to existing merchant integrations.

summary: JioOnePay is a payment aggregation platform: merchants integrate once instead of once per gateway.
A microservices architecture with an API Gateway (jop-gateway-service) as the single entry point routes and protects all traffic.
The core engineering challenge — many gateways, many formats — is solved with a per-gateway adapter layer around one standard internal model.
quickRevision: JioOnePay = payment aggregation platform by Jio Platforms.
Solves: merchants previously integrated individually with every gateway/bank.
Flow: customer picks payment mode → merchant calls JioOnePay REST API → routed to gateway/bank → response flows back.
Architecture: microservices + API Gateway pattern (jop-gateway-service, port 9985).
Backend stack: Core Java, Spring Boot, Spring Data JPA, Oracle DB, REST APIs.
Frontend stack: React, Next.js, TypeScript.
Key features: merchant onboarding, gateway integration, multiple payment modes, dynamic routing, refunds & monitoring.
Core challenge solved: a common abstraction layer normalizes every gateway's request/response format.

beginner: What problem does JioOnePay solve for merchants?
What payment modes does JioOnePay support?
What is an API Gateway, and which service plays that role in JioOnePay?
intermediate: How does JioOnePay handle the fact that every payment gateway has a different API format?
Walk through what happens end-to-end when a customer makes a UPI payment.
What was your specific role in building JioOnePay?
advanced: How would you design the abstraction layer that lets JioOnePay add a new payment gateway without affecting existing merchants?
What are the tradeoffs of a microservices + API Gateway architecture for a payment platform specifically?
How would you extend JioOnePay's architecture to support a new payment method end-to-end?

relatedTopics.label: Complete Payment Lifecycle — the full sequence diagram
Architecture Layers — the seven layers this system is built from

title: Complete Payment Lifecycle
overview: Before looking at individual services, it helps to see the **entire journey** a payment takes — from the merchant's first request to the merchant getting notified of the outcome.
This page walks through that full journey once, end-to-end, using JioOnePay's actual service names.
Every layer and service introduced later in this section is just a closer look at one step in this same journey.
why: Jumping straight into ~15 named microservices without first seeing the journey they're part of makes each one feel arbitrary.
Seeing the whole lifecycle once gives every later detail — a specific service, a specific layer — an obvious place to slot into.
problem: A payment isn't one request-response — it's a multi-step journey involving a redirect, a UI, a mode choice, and two independent completion signals.
Without seeing the full journey first, it's easy to assume a payment is confirmed the moment the customer's browser redirects back — which is not actually true here.

intro: The journey has three natural parts: getting the customer to the checkout page, processing their chosen payment mode, and confirming the outcome to everyone who needs to know.

topics.heading: Part 1 — Initiation to Checkout
points: The Merchant sends an initiate request; `jop-payment-link-service` validates it and stores the context in Redis under a token.
It returns an auto-submitting HTML form back to the merchant's page.
The merchant's browser auto-submits that form, which routes through the gateway to `jop-payment-options-service`.

The Options service looks up the token, fetches the merchant's configured payment modes, and redirects to the actual checkout UI — which calls back to the Options service the moment it renders, to load the dashboard.

topics.heading: Part 2 — Mode Selection to Gateway
points: The customer picks a mode (UPI, Card, Wallet, Net Banking) from the options shown.
That choice routes to a mode-specific service — `jop-upi-service`, `jop-ccdcnb-service`, or a wallet/other service.
Non-card modes call a `jop-core-*-service` adapter, which talks to the actual bank/gateway. Card payments instead go to the isolated `jop-pci-ccdc-card-service`, which calls the bank/ACS directly.

topics.heading: Part 3 — Completion & Notification
points: The bank/gateway sends back **two independent signals**: a browser redirect (to `jop-payment-b2b-service`) and a server-to-server callback (to `jop-payment-s2s-service`).
`jop-payment-s2s-service` is the authoritative one — it writes the final outcome to the Oracle transaction database and sends the merchant a webhook with a checksum.
`jop-payment-b2b-service` redirects the customer's browser back to the merchant, and typically also triggers an internal webhook to `jop-payment-s2s-service` to keep both paths in sync.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Merchant
    participant GW as jop-gateway-service
    participant PLS as jop-payment-link-service
    participant PGD as jop-core-pg-details-service
    participant R as Redis
    participant CHK as jop-payment-options-service
    participant MODE as Mode Service (UPI / CCDCNB / Wallet)
    participant CORE as jop-core-* / PCI service
    participant BANK as Bank / PG / ACS
    participant B2B as jop-payment-b2b-service
    participant S2S as jop-payment-s2s-service
    participant DB as Oracle Txn DB

    M->>GW: 1. Initiate payment request
    GW->>PLS: forward
    PLS->>PGD: validate checksum + config
    PLS->>R: store token to jopMsg
    PLS-->>M: htmlForm (Base64)
    M->>GW: 2. Browser auto-POST
    GW->>CHK: forward
    CHK->>R: lookup token
    CHK->>PGD: fetch configured modes
    CHK-->>M: redirect to Payment Options UI
    M->>CHK: 3. UI calls back on render
    M->>MODE: 4. Customer selects mode
    MODE->>CORE: forward (or direct to PCI service for cards)
    CORE->>BANK: 5. Process payment
    BANK-->>B2B: 6a. Browser return
    BANK-->>S2S: 6b. Server callback
    S2S->>DB: 7. Write transaction outcome
    B2B-->>M: 8a. Redirect customer
    S2S-->>M: 8b. Webhook plus checksum
architectureFlow.caption: The complete journey, using JioOnePay's real service names, from initiation through both completion paths.

simpleExample.scenario: A customer completes a ₹499 UPI payment, followed end-to-end.
simpleExample.code:
1. Merchant -> jop-payment-link-service: initiate
2. jop-payment-link-service -> Redis: store token
3. Merchant browser auto-POSTs -> jop-payment-options-service
4. Customer selects UPI on the checkout UI
5. jop-upi-service -> jop-core-<provider>-service -> Bank
6. Bank -> jop-payment-s2s-service (server callback)
7. jop-payment-s2s-service -> Oracle DB + merchant webhook
simpleExample.input: A single UPI payment request for a fixed amount.
simpleExample.execution: The request moves through initiation, checkout, mode selection, and gateway processing, exactly once, in order.
simpleExample.output: The merchant receives a webhook confirming SUCCESS; the customer sees a confirmation screen via the browser-redirect path.
simpleExample.explanation: Every payment mode follows this same overall shape — only the mode-selection and gateway-integration steps differ based on what the customer picked.

realWorldExample.scenario: The bank's server callback (S2S) arrives, but the customer's browser never redirects back — they closed the tab.
realWorldExample.code:
BANK -->> S2S: server callback (SUCCESS)
S2S ->> DB: write SUCCESS
S2S -->> Merchant: webhook + checksum

# Browser redirect (B2B) never happens - customer closed the tab
# Merchant still gets the correct outcome via the webhook
realWorldExample.input: A completed payment where the customer's browser never returns to the merchant's site.
realWorldExample.execution: The server-to-server callback still fires independently of the browser, since it's a direct bank-to-JioOnePay signal, not something that depends on the customer's browser at all.
realWorldExample.output: The merchant is correctly notified of the SUCCESS outcome via the webhook, even though the browser-redirect path never completed.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is exactly why the server callback (S2S), not the browser redirect (B2B), is the authoritative signal — the browser path can fail for reasons that have nothing to do with whether the payment actually succeeded.

comparisonTables.title: Browser Redirect (B2B) vs Server Callback (S2S)
rows: Get the customer back to the merchant's site
Yes — tab closed, network drop, etc.
Retried by the bank if delivery fails
The database write and the merchant webhook

advantages: Splitting the journey into initiation, mode selection, and completion makes each part independently understandable.
Having two independent completion signals means the transaction outcome doesn't depend on the customer's browser behaving well.
tradeoffs: Two independent completion paths (B2B and S2S) mean extra reconciliation logic is needed to keep the customer-facing and merchant-facing outcomes in sync.
bestPractices: Always treat the server-to-server callback as the source of truth — never the browser redirect alone.
Store enough context in Redis at initiation time that every later step (checkout, mode selection) can be resumed from just the token.
commonMistakes: Assuming a customer reaching the browser-redirect page means the payment succeeded — it only means the browser came back, not that the transaction did.
Treating the Payment Options UI's render-time call as optional — it's how the UI actually gets the merchant's configured modes.
interviewTips: When asked to describe your project, walk through this exact lifecycle in order — it demonstrates you understand the flow, not just a list of service names.
Be ready to explain, specifically, why the browser redirect isn't trusted as the confirmation of success.

faq.q: What happens if the browser redirect (B2B) never fires?
faq.a: The transaction is still correctly recorded and the merchant is still correctly notified, because the server callback (S2S) to jop-payment-s2s-service is independent of the browser — it's a direct signal from the bank.

faq.q: Why does the UI call back to jop-payment-options-service the moment it renders?
faq.a: That's how it fetches the actual data to display — the merchant's configured payment modes, fetched live rather than baked into the redirect itself.

faq.q: Is the token stored in Redis at initiation reused throughout the whole journey?
faq.a: Yes — it's what lets the Checkout Layer, and later steps, look up the original request's context (the jopMsg) without the merchant needing to resend it.

summary: A JioOnePay payment is a multi-step journey: initiation and checksum validation, a checkout redirect, mode selection, gateway processing, and two independent completion signals.
The server-to-server callback, not the browser redirect, is the authoritative confirmation of a payment's outcome.
Every service and layer covered next in this section is a closer look at one specific step in this same journey.
quickRevision: Initiation: jop-payment-link-service validates checksum/config, stores context in Redis under a token.
Checkout: merchant's browser auto-POSTs the returned HTML form; jop-payment-options-service serves the payment options UI.
The UI calls jop-payment-options-service on render to load its actual data (configured modes).
Mode selection routes to jop-upi-service / jop-ccdcnb-service / wallet, or to the isolated PCI service for cards.
The bank sends TWO signals: a browser redirect (jop-payment-b2b-service) and a server callback (jop-payment-s2s-service).
jop-payment-s2s-service is authoritative — it writes to the Oracle DB and sends the merchant's webhook.
jop-payment-b2b-service only handles the customer-facing redirect, and often also triggers an internal webhook to keep S2S in sync.

beginner: What are the main steps in a JioOnePay payment, from initiation to notification?
What is the difference between the browser redirect and the server callback?
Which signal is treated as authoritative — the browser redirect or the server callback?
intermediate: Why does JioOnePay store a token in Redis at initiation time?
Walk through what happens if a customer closes their browser tab before the redirect completes.
Why does the Payment Options UI call back to its service on render, instead of receiving everything in the redirect?
advanced: How would you design reconciliation logic to keep the B2B and S2S completion paths in sync?
What would break if the browser redirect were mistakenly treated as the authoritative confirmation?
How would you extend this lifecycle to support a brand-new payment mode end-to-end?

relatedTopics.label: Overview — where JioOnePay sits between Merchant and Gateway
Architecture Layers — each step in this journey, in depth

title: Architecture Layers
overview: JioOnePay's payment flow is organized into **seven layers**, each with one clear responsibility — from receiving the merchant's request to notifying them of the final outcome.
Understanding the system layer by layer, instead of all ~15+ payment-related microservices at once, is the only way the architecture actually makes sense.
Each layer maps to real running services — this isn't a generic textbook diagram, it's how JioOnePay is actually built.
why: A payment aggregator genuinely has this many moving parts — explaining them as one flat list of services hides *why* each one exists.

Layers group services by responsibility, so a new engineer, or an interviewer, can reason about one concern at a time: routing, checkout, mode selection, card security, gateway integration, completion.
problem: Without layering, ~15+ payment services look like an undifferentiated mess — impossible to reason about or explain clearly in an interview.
Card payments specifically need to be isolated for PCI-DSS compliance — mixing that into a generic "payment mode" layer would be a real security and audit problem.

intro: The seven layers, in the order a request actually passes through them.

topics.heading: Layer 1 — Edge Layer
points: **Purpose** — the single public entry point for all merchant traffic; nothing about payments is decided here, no business logic, no database.
**Services** — Load Balancer, `jop-gateway-service` (:9985) — Spring Boot 3.1.5, Java 17, Spring Cloud Gateway (WebFlux + Netty, reactive, non-blocking).

**Routing** — all routes live in one Zookeeper key, `config-properties` (Base64 JSON); `ApiRoutesLocatorService` builds the in-memory route table from it and `ZookeeperRefreshListener` auto-refreshes on change — no restart needed, and no Consul/Kubernetes discovery involved.
**Resilience order** — every request is checked by **Circuit Breaker**, then **Rate Limiter**, then **Bulkhead**, in that exact order, before being forwarded.

**Failure handling** — Circuit Breaker failures redirect to `/fallback` (only on HTTP 5xx); Rate Limiter and Bulkhead reject immediately with their own error instead. All three are tunable **per route** via a second Zookeeper key, `resilience-properties`.
topics.mermaidCaption: The exact order resilience checks run in, for every request the gateway receives.

topics.heading: Layer 2 — Payment Initiation Layer
points: **Purpose** — receives the merchant's initiate request, validates it, and hands back a checkout form.
**Services** — `jop-payment-link-service` (`POST /jop/initiate/payment/request`), `jop-core-pg-details-service` (merchant config/modes), Redis (token → jopMsg).

**Request Flow** — the merchant calls `jop-payment-link-service`, which validates a checksum and merchant config against `jop-core-pg-details-service`, stores the request context in Redis under a token, and returns an auto-submitting HTML form (Base64-encoded) back to the merchant.
**Failure handling** — an invalid checksum or unknown merchant config is rejected here, before any token is even created.
topics.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Merchant: initiate request"] --> B["jop-payment-link-service"]
    B --> C["jop-core-pg-details-service: validate checksum + config"]
    B --> D[("Redis: token to jopMsg")]
    B --> E["Return htmlForm (Base64) to Merchant"]
topics.mermaidCaption: The request is validated and stashed under a token before any UI is even shown.

topics.heading: Layer 3 — Checkout Layer
points: **Purpose** — renders the payment options page the customer actually sees and interacts with.
**Services** — `jop-payment-options-service` (`/payment-options` → redirect UI), the Payment Options UI itself.

**Request Flow** — the merchant's page auto-POSTs the htmlForm from Layer 2; `jop-gateway-service` forwards it to `jop-payment-options-service`, which looks up the token in Redis, fetches the merchant's dynamically configured modes, and redirects the browser to the UI. The UI itself calls back to the options service on render, to load the dashboard.
**Failure handling** — an expired or unknown token at this point means the checkout session itself is invalid, and the customer sees an error instead of a payment form.
topics.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Merchant Browser
    participant GW as jop-gateway-service
    participant CHK as jop-payment-options-service
    participant R as Redis
    participant UI as Payment Options UI
    M->>GW: auto-POST htmlForm
    GW->>CHK: forward
    CHK->>R: lookup token
    CHK-->>UI: redirect
    UI->>CHK: payment-dashboard request on render
topics.mermaidCaption: The UI calls back to the options service the moment it renders — that's how it gets the actual payment modes to display.

topics.heading: Layer 4 — Payment Mode Orchestration Layer
points: **Purpose** — routes the customer's chosen payment mode (UPI, Card, Net Banking, Wallet, and more) to the service that handles it.
**Services** — `jop-upi-service`, `jop-ccdcnb-service` (credit/debit card + net banking), plus wallet and other mode-specific services.
**Dynamic configuration** — payment modes aren't hardcoded; they're configured per-merchant in an admin portal, and the Checkout Layer only shows modes actually enabled for that merchant.

**Request Flow** — the UI calls the mode-specific service directly (via the gateway) once the customer picks a mode; card payments are handed off to the PCI Layer instead of following the standard path.
topics.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Customer selects mode"] --> B{"Which mode?"}
    B -->|"UPI"| C["jop-upi-service"]
    B -->|"Card / Net Banking"| D["jop-ccdcnb-service"]
    B -->|"Wallet / other"| E["Wallet service"]
    D -->|"Card specifically"| F["PCI Layer"]
topics.mermaidCaption: Mode selection branches here; card payments are handed off to the isolated PCI layer.

topics.heading: Layer 5 — PCI Layer
points: **Purpose** — the only layer allowed to touch raw card data (PAN), isolated so the rest of the system never has to be PCI-DSS scoped.
**Services** — `jop-pci-ccdc-card-service`, reached specifically for credit/debit card payments.

**Why it's isolated** — PCI-DSS compliance requires strict controls (encryption, access logging, network segmentation) around anything that touches a card number; keeping that inside one dedicated service means the other services never need those controls.

**Request Flow** — `jop-ccdcnb-service` hands card payments to `jop-pci-ccdc-card-service`, which calls the bank/ACS **directly** over HTTP — it bypasses the normal core-gateway-adapter path entirely, keeping the card-data path as short as possible.
topics.mermaid:
graph LR
    A["jop-ccdcnb-service"] -->|"CC/DC only"| B["jop-pci-ccdc-card-service"]
    B -->|"direct HTTP"| C["Bank / ACS"]
topics.mermaidCaption: Card data takes the shortest, most isolated path in the entire system — straight to the bank.

topics.heading: Layer 6 — Gateway Integration Layer
points: **Purpose** — talks to the actual external payment gateways and banks on behalf of every non-card payment mode.

**Services** — `jop-core-*-service` adapters, one per provider: Razorpay, PhonePe, Easebuzz, Citi, and others — each with its own **Apache HttpClient 5 wrapper**; there's no shared HTTP client abstraction across providers, since different banks have genuinely different requirements.
**Routing** — Zookeeper-based dynamic routing picks a provider by health, cost, or merchant preference.

**Security** — bank-grade integrations use **mTLS** — e.g. Citi loads a **PKCS12 client certificate** over **TLS 1.2**, so the bank verifies JioOnePay's identity too, not just the reverse. Timeouts, proxy settings, and certificate paths come from Zookeeper config, tunable per environment without a redeploy.

**Internal calls (how this layer is reached)** — services call each other via **Spring Cloud OpenFeign** (`@FeignClient(name = "service-name")`), resolved at call-time through Zookeeper-based service discovery — a plain Java interface that reads like a local method call. These calls go directly service-to-service and never pass through the Edge Layer's gateway, which is for external client traffic only.

**No message broker** — every call, internal or external, is a direct synchronous HTTP request/response; there's no Kafka or RabbitMQ anywhere in this system. `jop-common-redis-lib` provides shared caching (idempotency keys, session state) across services — that's caching, not messaging.

**Failure handling** — Spring Retry (`@Retryable`) layers on top of some Feign clients for transient internal failures; external retries are handled per-adapter. This synchronous, broker-less design is exactly why the Edge Layer's Circuit Breaker/Bulkhead exist — to contain a slow downstream call, since there's no async buffer to fall back on.
topics.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["jop-upi-service / jop-ccdcnb-service"] --> B["jop-core-razorpay-service"]
    A --> C["jop-core-phonepe-service"]
    A --> D["jop-core-citi-service"]
    B --> E["External PG / Bank"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
topics.mermaidCaption: One adapter per provider — new gateways are added here without touching upstream services.

topics.heading: Layer 7 — Completion Layer
points: **Purpose** — records the final outcome and tells everyone who needs to know: the customer's browser and the merchant's system.
**Services** — `jop-payment-b2b-service` (browser redirect back to the merchant) and `jop-payment-s2s-service` (server-to-server callback, database write, merchant webhook).

**Two separate completion paths** — the bank returns the customer's **browser** to `jop-payment-b2b-service` for the redirect, while a **server-to-server callback** goes to `jop-payment-s2s-service` independently; the two aren't the same request.

**Request Flow** — `jop-payment-s2s-service` writes the outcome to the Oracle transaction database and sends the merchant a webhook with a checksum; `jop-payment-b2b-service` redirects the customer's browser back to the merchant, and often also triggers an internal webhook to `jop-payment-s2s-service` to keep both paths in sync.
topics.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Bank / PG"] -->|"browser return"| B["jop-payment-b2b-service"]
    A -->|"server callback"| C["jop-payment-s2s-service"]
    B -.->|"internal webhook"| C
    C --> D[("Oracle Txn DB")]
    B --> E["Redirect customer to Merchant"]
    C --> F["Webhook + checksum to Merchant"]
topics.mermaidCaption: Two independent completion paths that converge on the same transaction record.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    L1["Layer 1: Edge"] --> L2["Layer 2: Payment Initiation"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3: Checkout"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4: Payment Mode Orchestration"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5: PCI"]
    L4 --> L6["Layer 6: Gateway Integration"]
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7["Layer 7: Completion"]
architectureFlow.caption: The seven layers in request order — each detailed individually above.

simpleExample.scenario: Tracing a single ₹499 UPI payment through all seven layers.
simpleExample.code:
1. Merchant -> Edge Layer (jop-gateway-service)
2. Edge -> Payment Initiation (jop-payment-link-service): checksum OK, token stored
3. Checkout Layer (jop-payment-options-service): renders UPI as an available mode
4. Payment Mode Orchestration: customer selects UPI -> jop-upi-service
5. Gateway Integration: jop-upi-service -> jop-core-<provider>-service -> Bank
6. Completion: server callback -> jop-payment-s2s-service -> DB + merchant webhook
simpleExample.input: A single UPI payment request for ₹499.
simpleExample.execution: The request passes through all seven layers exactly once, in order, each one adding exactly one piece of the puzzle.
simpleExample.output: The merchant receives a webhook confirming SUCCESS, and the customer sees a confirmation screen.
simpleExample.explanation: This is the same shape every payment mode follows — only Layer 4 and 5/6 differ based on which mode the customer picked.

realWorldExample.scenario: A card payment specifically, which routes through the isolated PCI Layer instead of the standard Gateway Integration path.
realWorldExample.code:
1-3. Same as UPI: Edge -> Payment Initiation -> Checkout
4. Payment Mode Orchestration: customer selects Card -> jop-ccdcnb-service
5. PCI Layer: jop-ccdcnb-service hands off to jop-pci-ccdc-card-service
6. jop-pci-ccdc-card-service calls the Bank/ACS DIRECTLY (bypasses jop-core-*-service adapters)
7. Completion: same as any other mode
realWorldExample.input: A card payment, where raw card data must be handled.
realWorldExample.execution: Layer 4 detects the mode is a card payment and routes to the PCI Layer instead of a standard core-gateway adapter; the PCI service talks to the bank directly, keeping the card-data path as short and isolated as possible.
realWorldExample.output: The transaction completes through a completely separate path for the card-handling step, converging back to the same Completion Layer as every other mode.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is exactly why the PCI Layer exists as its own thing — it's the one place in the whole system where card data flows, and it's designed to be as small and auditable as possible.

comparisonTables.title: Layer Details Reference
rows: Forwarded to mode service (or PCI Layer for cards)
Per-adapter retries, mTLS for bank-grade calls

comparisonTables.title: Circuit Breaker vs Rate Limiter vs Bulkhead (Layer 1: Edge)
Internal (Feign) vs External (Apache HttpClient + mTLS) — Layer 6
rows: mTLS (e.g. PKCS12 cert + TLS 1.2) for banks
Goes through the Edge Layer gateway?
No — direct from the owning microservice

advantages: Layering means a new engineer, or an interviewer, can reason about one responsibility at a time instead of ~15+ services at once.
Isolating card data into one PCI-scoped service keeps the other services entirely out of PCI-DSS audit scope.
tradeoffs: More layers means more network hops for a single payment — each layer boundary is a potential point of latency or failure.
Two independent completion paths (B2B and S2S) require careful reconciliation logic to keep them in sync, rather than one simpler path.
bestPractices: Isolate card data (PCI Layer) completely from every other payment mode — never let PAN flow through a shared/generic service.
Keep mode selection dynamic (admin-configured) rather than hardcoded, so enabling a new mode for a merchant is a config change, not a deployment.
Give the two completion paths (browser redirect vs server callback) independent responsibility — never assume one guarantees the other happened.
commonMistakes: Assuming the browser redirect (B2B) alone confirms a successful payment — only the server-to-server callback (S2S) is authoritative.
Letting card-handling logic leak outside the PCI Layer, expanding PCI-DSS audit scope unnecessarily.
Treating all payment services as one flat list instead of reasoning about them layer by layer.
interviewTips: When asked to explain this architecture, walk through the layers in request order — it's a much stronger answer than listing services alphabetically.
Be ready to explain specifically why card payments get their own isolated layer — this is a favorite senior-level follow-up.

faq.q: Why does the browser redirect and the server callback go to two different services?
faq.a: The browser redirect (B2B) is for the customer's experience — getting them back to the merchant's site. The server callback (S2S) is the authoritative, machine-to-machine confirmation that's actually trusted for recording the transaction and notifying the merchant. Relying on the browser redirect alone would be unreliable — a customer can close the tab before it completes.

faq.q: Why is there a separate PCI Layer instead of handling cards inside the normal Gateway Integration Layer?
faq.a: PCI-DSS compliance requires strict controls around anything that touches raw card data. Isolating that into one dedicated service means only that one service needs the heavy compliance controls — everything else in the system stays out of PCI scope entirely.

faq.q: How does the Checkout Layer know which payment modes to show a specific merchant?
faq.a: It's not hardcoded — modes are configured per-merchant in an admin portal, and jop-payment-options-service fetches that configuration live from jop-core-pg-details-service before rendering the checkout page.

faq.q: Why is the Edge Layer gateway built on Spring Cloud Gateway (WebFlux) instead of a traditional servlet-based gateway?
faq.a: Spring Cloud Gateway is reactive and non-blocking (built on WebFlux + Netty), which lets a single gateway instance handle many concurrent in-flight requests — including slow downstream calls — without needing a thread per request.

faq.q: Why use Feign instead of a raw RestTemplate or WebClient for internal calls in Layer 6?
faq.a: Feign lets you declare a REST client as a plain Java interface — you call it like a local method, and Feign plus Zookeeper-based discovery handle resolving the real address and making the HTTP call, cutting out repetitive client boilerplate.

summary: JioOnePay's payment flow is organized into seven layers: Edge, Payment Initiation, Checkout, Payment Mode Orchestration, PCI, Gateway Integration, and Completion.
Each layer has one clear responsibility, and card payments specifically get isolated into their own PCI layer for compliance.
The Completion layer has two independent paths — browser redirect and server callback — with the server callback being the authoritative source of truth.
quickRevision: 7 layers in request order: Edge → Payment Initiation → Checkout → Payment Mode Orchestration → (PCI | Gateway Integration) → Completion.
Edge: jop-gateway-service, port 9985, routes + protects.
Payment Initiation: jop-payment-link-service validates checksum/config, stores token in Redis, returns an HTML auto-submit form.
Checkout: jop-payment-options-service serves the payment options UI, fetching dynamically configured modes.
Payment Mode Orchestration: routes to jop-upi-service / jop-ccdcnb-service / wallet, based on admin-configured modes per merchant.
PCI Layer: jop-pci-ccdc-card-service is the only service allowed to touch card PAN, calling the bank/ACS directly.
Gateway Integration: one jop-core-*-service adapter per provider (Razorpay, PhonePe, Easebuzz, Citi), each with its own HttpClient wrapper — no shared abstraction across providers.
Completion: jop-payment-b2b-service (browser redirect) and jop-payment-s2s-service (server callback, DB write, merchant webhook) are two independent paths.
No message broker anywhere — every call, internal (Feign + Zookeeper discovery) or external (per-adapter HttpClient + mTLS), is direct synchronous HTTP.
jop-common-redis-lib = shared caching (idempotency keys, session state), not messaging. jop-common-lib = shared Feign/Zookeeper/Retry setup.
